In FY2024, we carried out projects in collaboration with our suppliers, in which we contributed about £ , PRINCIPLE: FAIR BUSINESS DEALINGS WITH OUR SUPPLIERS Despite Fairtrade certification and other certification schemes being a useful tool, and a reassuring mark of producers and traders doing the right thing, Lush as a company does not solely rely on these certifications. We find that working as directly as possible with primary producers of our ingredients and packaging is the best way for us to achieve transparency in our supply network, while also making sure that our supplier relationships are fair and built on open communication. The term fair trade, whilst associated with formal certifications, can be expressed and verified in many forms, some of which are far more economically and structurally viable for our producers. For example, we work in collaboration with suppliers who choose to demonstrate fair trade practices in alternative ways that don’t align with the certification standards currently available. This includes projects such as a Participatory Guarantee System, or verifying the price paid directly to farmers ourselves. Currently, we have materials from suppliers in the UK that are either organic or fair trade certified or both. Across these suppliers, different organic certification schemes are used, as well as different fair trade certification schemes.

The term fair trade, whilst associated with formal certifications, can be expressed and verified in many forms, some of which are far more economically and structurally viable for our producers. For example, we work in collaboration with suppliers who choose to demonstrate fair trade practices in alternative ways that don’t align with the certification standards currently available. This includes projects such as a Participatory Guarantee System, or verifying the price paid directly to farmers ourselves. Currently, we have materials from suppliers in the UK that are either organic or fair trade certified or both. Across these suppliers, different organic certification schemes are used, as well as different fair trade certification schemes. This information is most reliable for the UK as globally we track this data in different ways, however we are in the process of aligning this so we can better report on our organic and fair trade certifications globally. PRINCIPLE: FAIR BUSINESS DEALINGS WITH OUR SUPPLIERS As with a lot of global businesses, our supply networks are complex and involve a number of different processes. While trying to source as directly as possible to un-complicate things, it is not always an option, and a lot of our networks are made up of several tiers stretching over numerous countries. We learnt early on in our journey that it is vital to gain an understanding of each material, from who is involved in its supply to the local impacts of its production, in order to ensure top quality ingredients with no exploitation at any stage. Our preference is always to see, for ourselves, how our suppliers work and our buyers and buying support teams conduct regular in person visits with our top priority suppliers so we can do just that.

Principle: Naked Products Evolving Principle: Our goal of minimising the use of packaging means that we will always strive to invent and perfect naked products. PRINCIPLE: NAKED PRODUCTS At Lush we call our products that don’t need any packaging Naked. Smell that? It’s the scent of products without their packaging on. That’s right, years of smelling us before you see us is rooted in our naked ethos. Packaging is one of the biggest sustainability issues facing the cosmetics industry today. Imagine if we could eliminate the need for packaging altogether? This is the challenge we pose to our Inventors and R&D teams, whose legacy has given us the shampoo bar, massage bar, naked deodorant, henna brick, naked body conditioner, naked shower gel and more soap than you could ever dream of. Naked products also tend to last longer than their packaged counterparts because they are more concentrated and have minimal water content. Tests with users tell us that one shampoo bar can last the equivalent washes as three 200ml bottles of liquid shampoo.

Packaging is one of the biggest sustainability issues facing the cosmetics industry today. Imagine if we could eliminate the need for packaging altogether? This is the challenge we pose to our Inventors and R&D teams, whose legacy has given us the shampoo bar, massage bar, naked deodorant, henna brick, naked body conditioner, naked shower gel and more soap than you could ever dream of. Naked products also tend to last longer than their packaged counterparts because they are more concentrated and have minimal water content. Tests with users tell us that one shampoo bar can last the equivalent washes as three 200ml bottles of liquid shampoo. Not only are we saving those three bottles worth of plastic, we are also having to ship less product and less weight, reducing C02 emissions. The full report will take you through how we arrived at these estimations. Our initial interest in making naked products was born out of necessity. After the demise of Cosmetics To Go, the Lush Co-Founders simply didn’t have the money to invest in packaging, but this actually sparked a revolutionary approach to a growing industry problem. We have been selling solid products even in the years before Lush, when we were known as Cosmetics To Go.

The mail order company would wrap exciting bath bombs, bubble bars, bath oils, soaps, massage bars and the now famous shampoo bar in sometimes extravagant packaging! It was only when Lush opened its first store – in Poole High Street with a delicatessen vibe – that those products went naked. Our latest invention is the Naked Mascara, formulated with butters and waxes to create an iconic, solid product. This has become a favourite product for our customers and has won multiple awards such as the 2022 UK Sustainable Beauty Awards for Sustainable Product, the 2023 NA Harper’s Bazaar Beauty Icons Awards for Most Innovative Beauty Product and the 2024 Australia Sustainability Awards for the Product Award.

We commit to using conflict material free commodity hardware that has high output but a low energy consumption powered by renewable green energy across the group, where possible. We ensure that all of Lush’s staff and customer data is encrypted, secure and transparent. Our Customers and Staff have the right to know what information we hold about them. PRINCIPLE: DIGITAL ETHICS Lush Digital believes technology should give more than it takes from society and the environment. We champion ethical hardware, ethical data, ethical design, and open source technologies and we support and elevate communities who feel the same. Just as Lush believes in ethically sourced ingredients, our Digital business believes in ethical tech that gives back to the community. To keep us accountable to the beliefs that we hold true as a technological business, Lush Digital formulated its own set of Digital Ethics Policies to consider every time we design, build or launch a digital product. They can be consolidated into three categories: Open Source, Ethical Hardware and Ethical Data. Our open source policy aims to equally contribute to open-source tech communities, wherever we can, in order to support the grassroots world of creating tech for everyone to share and benefit from without the heavy burden of cost. We are committed to the principles of open source and in that spirit, we strive to use technologies with OSI-approved licenses and to contribute our research and code back to open communities under such licenses.

Lush Lens on the Lush App allows customers to scan naked products and get information about ingredients, how to use the product and legal requirements normally found on packaging. In March 2023, we announced a drive to divest away from the ‘big-tech’ players Amazon, Google, Facebook, Apple and Microsoft. Similar to our open source policy, we’re taking back control by favouring the grassroots and communities over large corporations who only hoard profit. This announcement is the next step in our distancing from big tech after we removed ourselves from Instagram, Facebook, Snapchat and TikTok, and launched our Anti-Social Media Policy in November 2021. → £ .4M £ .6M % reduction in big tech spending THE DIVESTMENT JOURNEY FY23: £ .4M ( % platforms, % Google ads) FY24: £ .0M ( % reduction) FY25: £ .6M ( % total reduction) We invested £ million in our R&D efforts in Tech Warriors and a further £ .5m in new projects in Digital in FY2025. Principle: Renewable Energy Evolving Principle: The past reliance on fossil fuels and nuclear for the world’s energy needs has caused problems. We will seek out and favour advances in new energy sources and solutions as they become available. PRINCIPLE: RENEWABLE ENERGY In 2024, we've hit a major milestone: % renewable electricity across Lush operations (excluding partner markets). Through a mix of direct ‘deep green’ tariffs — % of properties — and standard electricity tariffs with matching impact Renewable Energy Certificate purchases, many with additional benefits such as community solar, including pollinator-friendly habitats or additional consideration for the impact potential on animals. The heat challenge remains trickier.

The heat challenge remains trickier. We're almost fossil gas-free in retail and gradually electrifying factories. Our North American sites run on % renewable energy despite still utilising gas, by supporting recovery of natural gas from a landfill project. In the UK,our main R&D building (Unit Witney) and our EC’s/Perfumes factory operate fully on electricity with Unit also being supplied by 126kWp of solar PV directly from the roof, supplying over % of our annual electricity demand. Our Japanese manufacturing facility is already % electrified. We are currently performing energy audits in Japan, LED upgrades across UK facilities, remaining non-LED German shops, better monitoring, double doors in the new Glasgow store to improve efficiency, and ‘power down’ campaigns for staff.

PRINCIPLE: RECYCLED MATERIALS When packaging is absolutely necessary, we make it count. We've been using recycled plastic since 2008 in the UK. We recently switched the colour additives from virgin to recycled plastic for black pots in the UK and are rolling this out to other markets, so we can claim % recycled pots. Most of our pesky pumps, virgin until recently, now have - % recycled content. Perfume spritzers, Shield & Willow and the 100ml Super Milk are still a work in progress. For our % recycled PET bottles, we went further and sourced Prevented Ocean Plastic for most markets (not in North America yet). This comes from bottle collectors working 50km from water bodies, helping prevent bottles reaching the ocean.

Principle: Environmental Policies Evolving Principle: Lush will always seek to challenge itself over all its uses of the world’s resources. We aim to reduce and minimise our impact with policies that meet the needs of a changing and constantly evolving world situation. We will challenge and target ourselves in areas such as air transport, energy use, materials into landfill, recycling rates, pollution and waste outputs. PRINCIPLE: ENVIRONMENTAL POLICIES As a business making physical products, our environmental footprint is still unavoidable — from materials and energy to carbon emissions and waste. What we can do is keep our footprint in check, align with natural cycles through circular economy and regenerative principles, and challenge ourselves to use less while staying within planetary boundaries. Our Carbon Footprint Total emissions , tCO2e in FY24 (vs , tCO2e in FY22)—roughly equivalent to Mount Etna erupting for days straight.

PRINCIPLE: ENVIRONMENTAL POLICIES Our Water Footprint ,081m³ total water use tracked globally in FY24—equivalent to over million baths. Manufacturing accounts for one-third, retail two-thirds. Median retail water use intensity: .4m³/m² annually, which we're working to reduce through engaging with stores with higher water footprints and operational improvements. Only % of shops have actual water meters (the rest rely on landlord estimates), so we're investigating meter installation in water-stressed areas like LA, UAE, Spain, and Italy. Our supply chain faces increasing water risks—we lost our entire Bulgarian chamomile crop to drought in 2021, while flooding disrupted Indian Davana oil and Croatian grains. Smart stewardship means balancing customer experience with conservation: only % of bath sales occur in high water-stress regions, and shower products outsell bath products in water-stressed countries. PRINCIPLE: ENVIRONMENTAL POLICIES We're investing in water treatment at manufacturing sites—Japan upgraded to enzyme-enhanced purification, Toronto uses physical-chemical separation, and the UK is installing a second dissolved air flotation system.

When it comes to product waste, if the product is still usable we make every effort to donate it. We donate straight from the store or from our manufacturing centres to various charities. If it’s not possible to donate the product then we send these to composting where we can. In the UK, we invested in our own recycling centre, the Green Hub. It processes the Bring it Back returns from the stores and our UK manufacturing wastes.
